Transcript:
[music]
>> Hey everyone, welcome back to Make It
Happen Mondays where we talk about
sales, business, entrepreneurship,
personal growth, mental health, and
everything in between with guests who I
truly respect and I think make a
positive impact on the world around us.
Today I sat down with Julie Averill.
Julie was the global CIO at Lululemon
where she helped [music] scale the
company from 2 billion to over 10
billion in revenue. Before that, she was
the first ever CIO at REI and spent a
decade at Nordstrom's running the omni
channel transformation that
accidentally drove a quarter of a
billion dollars in revenue in 3 [music]
months.
She's now the founder and CEO of Gold
Thread and has a new book coming out on
June 16th called Chief Impact Officer.
Real transformation comes from humans,
not just artificial intelligence.
Now, I got to be honest, I was expecting
a polished CIO playbook conversation and
this was way more than that. We started
with her mom who was put in an orphanage
as a kid and protested by refusing to
speak for an entire year, which [music]
is awesome. And her dad, a major league
catcher, who taught her system thinking
from behind home plate.
Then we got into Lululemon story
everybody's heard in headlines, but
nobody's heard the way she tells it.
She was talking about how she was
sitting on her couch 2 days before her
first
her first day and clicked on a product
page and saw a yoga pose error message
[music] and realized the entire site was
down. Turns out the data center had been
sold and nobody knew it. And nobody
owned it, and that was the problem with
the culture [music] there from an IT
perspective. Uh 20 hours of outage, her
first email to her new team came from
her Gmail account, which is brutal for a
new CIO to come in that way. But, lot to
fix and she did. Then we got into some
stuff that I've been truly obsessing
over recently. Stuff like why legacy
SaaS should be genuinely scared right
now. Why AI is a magnifying glass, not a
solve-all.
Why if you try to automate a broken
process that takes 30 people in a room
to make a decision, AI is going to
expose that dysfunction faster than fix
it. And we talked about how radical
honesty and trust are the only way to
unlock real innovation in a team because
without trust people will hide how
brittle the systems really are, which is
an absolute fact. If you're in tech,
retail, SaaS, or you're a leader trying
to figure out where AI actually fits
inside your business instead of bolting
it on as a shiny dashboard, this one's
for you. I hope you enjoy the
conversation as much as I did. Let's
make it happen.
Julie Averill, welcome to the Make It
Happen Monday podcast. How you doing
today?
>> I'm great. Thanks, John. I'm happy to be
here.
>> Yeah, I'm excited. I mean, we have You
got a very interesting career and a lot
going on these days and we're going to
get to all of that. Specifically, you
know, transformation, technology, CIO,
and all the advisory work that you're
doing with the world of AI right now,
which is just bananas.
Uh
personally, never seen things move as
fast as I've seen right now. So, I think
a lot of your advice is going to be
pretty
timely for a lot of people. But before
we did it get into that, I always love
to get a little bit of background on
you. Like where, you know, the stuff
that we can't find on LinkedIn. You
know, where did you come from? How How
did you become you? You know, parents,
your upbringing, those type of fun

from a pretty tough background where she
was

when parents didn't get divorced and she
was put in a

didn't want kids anymore. So, she and
her brother

>> were put in a an orphanage. But my mom,
being my mom, her form of protest was
she didn't speak for an entire year.
They thought that she was mute. She
wasn't. She was just extremely stubborn
and being rebellious. So, that's the
thing you need to know about my mom is
that she came across as really quiet and
but there was a steel underneath her and
>> that.
>> So, she married my dad with her parents
pushed her into marrying him when you
know, she was 16 years old. He his
She got married at 18, but she met him
at 16. His father was in um was a
baseball player. So,
famous man around town. Her parents saw
him coming a mile away was like, this is
the guy. Like this is your ticket out.
And luckily luckily for her, it was a
match made in heaven. They were just so
in love married for 60 years.
And uh it was you know, it was a
beautiful story. My dad everyone thought
that my dad was the tough guy. He played
baseball uh in the majors for seven or
eight years.
Um but the two of them together were
quite a combo and so I grew up from my
dad
who's was a catcher in the majors.
>> Mhm.
>> [clears throat]
>> And catchers have a unique position,
right? They're the one person that is
looking out at the entire field while
everyone else is looking at the batter
and what they're going to do. But my dad
was keenly observant and he could tell
you back on his career who he hit
against, who threw what, who you know,
he just knew the ins and outs of
baseball.
>> Mhm.
>> And so his like I describe what the way
he looked at the world in terms of a
systems thinker.
>> Yeah, so it's what he saw.
>> Yeah, he saw what was coming ahead of
time. You go to a Mariners game with him

going to pitch this guy inside and watch
what's going to happen. He's going to
pop it up. Or he's going to hit it he's
going to hit it uh you know, home run
down the left field line." And it just
amazed me how often he was right because
he knew the people.

understood the psychology.

shop in Tacoma, Washington.

she, you know, she did the payroll. And
she took it until one day she asked him
for a raise. He gave her a 25 cent an
hour raise, which still didn't make her
paid what anyone anyone walking off the
street was paid.

cuz my heart said I wanted to study
computer science. I said, "Okay, I'll
study computer science, but I'm going to
get an MBA right afterwards." So, to me
that was the balance and a deal with
myself that let me feel good about
studying computer science. I had no idea
where that was going to take me.

it's, you know, you're you're great one

know, these days if you get one bad
press release, you could all of a

that really got you fired up?
>> What I love about retail is that you can
see the entire system from a product
being designed to it to it being
manufactured,
the supply chain that it takes to get
that into the right places, the planning
that it takes,
to the point of selling to the customer,
right? The choices that you make
online or in store affect whether a
customer wants to buy that or not. And
then all the way through

cycle is something that
I can just, you know, wrap my head
around. And particularly when we think
about technology, technology is involved
in every stage of that.
And so the, [clears throat]
you know, the seat that someone has in

>> Yeah, I think that's So, from I forget,

Nordstrom cuz I I forgot to write those

>> Yeah, I came in to Nordstrom after
spending time in the early dot com boom.
And I came into Nordstrom and I was
hired to help integrate this
100-year-old brick-and-mortar retailer
with a shiny Nordstrom.com that they had
just purchased back from investors.
>> Okay.
>> And so they they purchased it, they they
merged it with their catalog business.
>> Okay.
>> And so my job was to help bring, you
know, technically bring those two
together. And so one of the things you
obviously want to bring together was
inventory systems. What we didn't
realize was really the power of a
combined inventory until we went live
with store fulfill in in the end of
2013. And what that did was it turned
every Nordstrom store into a fulfillment
center. So all the merchandise that was
available in the store all of a sudden
became available online.
>> Nice.
>> And that in that year, the last three
months of the year, we accidentally
drove a quarter of a billion dollars in
revenue.
>> Ooh. Accidentally.
>> We accidentally did through, you know,
through the shared inventory and turning
the stores into fulfillment center. So
um
I ended up leading what we now know the
omni-channel
initiatives and particular the the store
fulfillment then store technologies. We
did a lot of innovative things like
mobile POS,
things that were really exploratory in
that time. You know, how does RFID fit
into this picture? Just innovations both
online in stores, marketing, everything
about the customer. So
it was a really fun time cuz we were,
you know, we were figuring it out. Now
it's like, oh you did omni-channel.
Okay, everybody does omni-channel.
>> [snorts]
>> Well, and that's actually so a lot
there's a lot of
similarities with what's going on right
now with the AI boom, right? So the
dot-com boom, none of us knew what was
going on. It was we were all figuring it
out. And you know, and and people were
slow to change quite frankly because you
I remember even back then people thought
it was a fad. The internet was not a
thing, it's going to go away. And so,
what were I guess looking back, what
were some of the takeaways that you
think are still applicable today in that
transformation in such a flux
environment that we're all in? What were
some of the specifics that you took away
from the Nordstrom experience that you
think either you or others in your
position could apply in in this
fast-paced like 10x times, you know, it
feels like that we're moving a thousand
times faster than we did back then, even
though back then it felt like we were
moving a thousand miles an hour.
>> Yeah. Yeah. Well, if you remember back
to that time, you could slap dot com on
the end of a company name and instantly
be worth millions, at least on paper.
>> Sure.
>> And that was because there was a, you
know,
everybody knew that it was something
big, but they didn't necessarily know
what it meant to their business. And so,
through that, right, through the dot com
bust,
the market sorted itself out and said,
"Okay, it's really not about
having a just having a website or
e-commerce, but how does your business
transform
through this new technology, right? So,
you know, Amazon emerged, for example,
right? So, the the market changed once
people understood how this new
technology actually fit into their
business. And I think we're seeing the
same thing right now. Like, how many
companies do you know that end in dot
AI?
>> Uh-huh. Yeah.
>> Right. It's not about the technology.
So, it's about, and I I see this a ton
right now of companies trying to just do
AI.
>> Uh-huh.
>> Right. What's your [clears throat]
What's your AI strategy? How many pilots
are you doing? What's, you know,
um
and they're focusing on the technology.
And I think the focus really needs to be
on the business.
>> Uh-huh.
>> Right. Understanding, where do you want
your business to go, and how can AI help
you get there faster or in a different
way?
So, I think that's a big lesson that I
just Yeah, it feels like we're déjà
vu-ing all over again in that that it's
you know, there's a lot of um I mean,
don't get me wrong. AI is amazing. It's
transformational to our lives, our
industry, etc. But, when you look at it
in a company, what is it still that that
company does
that makes them unique, and how does how
does technology help them accelerate
their their goals there?
>> [snorts]
>> Yeah, and I think that's one of the
things I'm struggling with right now,
you know, just even from a sales
perspective, right? Because
>> [sighs]
>> I think in in
tech sales at least, I you know, the
SaaS boom, if you will, you know, 2010
to 2020, give or take, or 2020-2022,
we got away from a lot of the
fundamentals because it was just quite
frankly, it was pretty easy, right? We
could send out sequences, and you know,
whatever, anybody with a pulse was
taking a demo call, and all that other
stuff. So, what I'm looking at here is,
okay, how do we get back to fundamentals
so that we can have that foundation to
build from? But, my question that I'm
struggling with is, what are the
fundamentals anymore?
So, as that relates to business,
what are the same fundamentals back then
that you still think are still relevant
today in business, and which ones do you
think have changed?
>> The fundamentals are different, right,
for every company, but
what's your company's strategy? And what
are the capabilities that you need to be
able to execute on that strategy? So,
I'll use Lululemon for as an example,
right? So, when I started, the company
was 2 billion. When I left, it was 10
billion. Uh so, 5x in 8 years.
What got us there was great product,
uh you know, great relationships with
the customers, with the guests,
um
solid supply chain, you know,
functioning organizations that were all
working together and doing that part.
Now, technology enabled all of that,
right? So, it couldn't have happened
without technology, but technology was
not the point. Like, my job was to serve
the strategy of the company. And so, I
think when companies today are thinking
about how does AI change them, you got
to look at what are your fundamentals.
>> Mhm.
>> And and how and how does the technology
help you get to where you want to get
to, to your strategy in a different way.
>> Mhm.
What's changed? So, like if if some of
the core fund you know,
customer success, having a good product,
supply chain, you know, uh alignment,
and all that stuff. It has there
anything Have you Have you seen any
anything fundamentally change about how
businesses need to look at things
different now than they ever did?
>> I think I'll I'll start with retail.
>> Okay.
>> Um so, top of the funnel,
>> Right.
>> So, we it used to be predictable. And
I'll go back in time, right? Used before
e-commerce, predictable.
>> Mhm.
>> Customers would walk into the stores,
they would look at what you had, they
made a decision to buy or not, they
paid, they walked out.
>> Mhm.
>> E-commerce comes in, now they have a
choice to research online, then go into
a store, buy all online, or still go
into a store.
Right? But, you controlled
that top of the funnel. You controlled
as a retailer how you presented your
products to the customers, whichever
avenue they came in.
And now what's changed is with AI,
there's channels that people are coming
from, like
conversational AI, for example, where
you can't control.
Right? You don't know how Claude or Chat
GPT is going to present your products in
in what order. And so, we just don't
know that yet.
>> Mhm.
>> Right.
And so,
the
the companies, I think, that are going
to survive this are the ones that really
understand
how they are truly different. So,
you know, what is it that makes your
your company special? If you're just
selling a widget that 10 other people
are selling, then you better have the
best price. You better have the best
widget, and you better have it at the
best price. But, if there's if you're a
brand that has uh
you know, has loyalty
to your brand, that that people are in
community with your brand, um
how do you hold on to those things? So,
when people are conversing with their
favorite AI engine, they're asking
specifically for your brand. And then,
you're able to take it from there.
>> So, from that perspective, is
brands,
community, and kind of your that the the
marketing side of the house
as if not more important? Cuz I've
always said like, from a sales
standpoint, right?
>> [snorts]
>> Um you have the best product in the
world, but if you can't sell it, it
doesn't matter. And so, I I've always
said, you know, you your product has to
do what it's expected to do, okay? Like,
you can't these days you can't show up
to the table without it doing
functionally what it's supposed to do.
Um but, is do you have to be the best
product right now and have the best
brand? Or, if you create that loyalty,
that brand, that thing that that your
you know, your audience will
accept something that isn't the best?
>> I think it depends where you put
yourself on the commodity scale.
>> Okay.
>> Right? So, if you if you are
hot in a high commodity competitive
environment, right? Then it's it's
quality and price.
>> Yeah.
>> Right? But if you're differentiated, if
you have you know, you're one of a kind,
right? Then know really well what makes
you unique

>> and hold on to that.
>> See, I feel like that's going to be so

hard.

how do you how have you helped Let's

you said first of all that that's told
told you everything you needed to know
about what you were walking yourself
into.
Um so walk me through that ex- like what
did it tell you, A, and B,
you know, what was unique about
Lululemon and what can other brands pull
from that to say like how did you go
through your process of what makes us
unique? We're selling yoga pants. I
mean, I'm obviously from a generic
consumer standpoint. You know, I was
like, "Whatever, these things are
stretchy pants and they're nice clothes
and there's a million of them. I can go
to Nordstrom's. I can go all these all
all these places." So what was How did
you identify what was unique about
Lululemon
uh so that you could lean in on that?
>> Um and you know, I was a tech person, so
I don't get full credit for this, but
I'll tell you I'll tell you my story
about before I started there. So 2 days
before I started, I'm sitting on my
couch at home. I'm
you know, shopping, pull up
lululemon.com, click on a product page
and get a an error message of a
yoga figure it like
in Savasana saying, "Temporary Savasana,
we're usually awesome at this."
>> [laughter]
>> And um
so I thought, "Well, that doesn't that
doesn't sound good." I clicked on
another one, same thing. Turns out the
entire website was down. I was the one
that discovered it. My team's very first
email to me
was from a Gmail account
reporting the website outage.
>> Woah.
>> Turns out that the site had been fully
hosted, fully outsourced to another
company. That company had sold it to
another company. So sold the data
center. So there was a data center,
entire data center that had gone down
and nobody who owned it knew that was
hosting the Lululemon website.
So the site was down for 20 hours.
>> What? Just for context here, um how how
many years in business was Lululemon and
what were their revenues at this point?
Just to get a good
>> Revenue revenue was a little bit under
um a little bit under 2 billion.
>> Okay.
>> And um
uh
20 years.
>> Ooh. So this isn't like, "Oh, we just
started and it we had a glitch here."
This is like established.
>> No.
>> Been in business for
>> countries.
>> 2 billion. Okay.
>> Um yeah.
But why I say that it was super
revealing, I knew everything that I
needed to know
um was because there was no culture of
ownership.
Right? The team had no ability to
monitor.
They had no escalation path. They they
didn't Yeah, they didn't know who to
call.
Um
and what that revealed, the lack of
ownership, was that there was a
fear-based culture. So
nobody really wanted to talk about how
brittle the systems were for fear of
being blamed for it.
And so when you ask what was special
about Lululemon, um you know, the
technology team was in a a state of
needing a lot of repair. But the thing
that drew me to Lululemon was the
culture
of we can do anything.
Like I never I had never seen a company
that just had this
um this desire to keep going
collectively. Everybody I talked to had
this curiosity and this um
it's almost no fear, right? Well, of
course we can do that. We're Lululemon.
Uh now, the technology team had had paid
the price for a lot of that because they
were trying to put everything together
and you know, we could talk all about
what that transformation looked like.
But really I think what made it special
was this DNA of innovation and curiosity
that was super magnetic. Right? That's
that's why I joined because I could tell
that it was a special company to be a
part of.
>> So [snorts] was it different so because
that culture of innovation and we can do
anything conflicts a little bit with
this the no ownership, right? Like I
don't I'm scared. So was was IT was the
was the culture of IT different than the
culture of Lululemon because you said
they kind of they were the ones who you
know, had to deal with all this stuff.
So were they somewhat insulated when you
came on board? Like why was there that
that difference?
>> Yeah, and I think this happens in a lot
of retail companies. Um I remember one
time I asked um
one of the executives at Nordstrom for
career advice. I said, "Look, I'm I
really love this company and I want to
know what my opportunities are." And,
uh, he said, "Julie, we think of IT like
we think of, um,
of, uh,
like the broom closet. So, when
something's broken, we go in there, we
rattle things around until it starts
working again, then we turn the lights
off,
and, uh, and we we go back to work. So,
if you want if you want a career in a
retail company, you've got to get into
either merchandising or stores."
So, that was my pep talk and I thought
this is where I'm going to make my
career.
>> There you go. Let's go. [laughter]
>> But, uh, you know, a lot of This is how
it was in retail, right? That
before we understood or before we were
able to really articulate how important
technology is to unlocking the strategy.
And at Nordstrom, what happened,
obviously, I told you about already, but
when we went live with store fulfill,
all of a sudden the executive teams were
highly interested in this technology
thing. The vocabulary changed. They
started asking about, "What are our fill
rates? What are our cancel rates? How do
we, you know, like
it changed from being a back office
function to a strategic enabler. And so,
at Lululemon, it hadn't gone through
that that yet. It was still seen as
really,
you know, that broom closet of, "Here,
we need some stuff. We're going to tell
them what we need. We're going to get
some stuff out, and then we're going to
go do the real work of the company."
>> Yeah. That's how I So, I spent about a
large portion of my early career in IT
services. So, I was one sales guy within
50 engineers, and so I know the I know
the how clients looked at it, and it was
almost it was it was so it was so tough
because
you never got praised. I mean, it the
the expectation was the system worked,
right? Like the expectation was I got my
emails when I got my emails that I could
access the folders when I could access
the folders. So, the only time at their
name ever our name ever came up was when
something went wrong.
And you know, that was a thing that that
the engineers I think obviously knew
getting into the profession, but it was
always something from my standpoint,
from a sales standpoint being like,
"Wait a minute. Like after a while, I'm
like, you got to throw an attaboy every
once in a while at them for for
[laughter] the fact that we've had no
outages here in a in a couple of months
with your system. Like, can't you,
right?" Or like find some way of
reminding the client that the reason
that there are no issues is because
we're doing our job. So, that [snorts]
that's always I I think I could see that
being a tough uh
mentality to have, quite frankly. Or or
or
yeah, just being in that environment,
not getting the kudos and always getting
the blame.
>> Yeah, especially if you are, you know,
like what we call behind the drywall.
>> Yeah. Yeah.
>> Right. So, I think it's changed since,
you know, like if you look at the
technology that we put in place in those
eight years that I was there, it was
remarkable and I built
team in China to, you know, like all the
unique technology and needs of the
customer in China. Um
you know, all of the global
infrastructure, the websites, the
merchandising systems, the retail
systems, the personalization and the
websites, right? All of that enabled the
growth. And so,
um you know, we were lucky to have
really good business partners who
understood how important the technology
was to um you know, to to for them to
reach their goals. And so, it was a
partnership. And I think the, you know,
the kudos came particularly to the teams
that were building new functionality.
But yeah, the network team, um
you know, they're still
>> It's happening. Yeah, the phone lights
up when something doesn't doesn't work
well.
>> So, you know, staying with Lululemon,
you talked about this culture of radical
honesty, right? Where So, you know, and
I I actually did a little bit of a stint
with Jack Welch back in the day when
when he started his online MBA program.
So, I worked with him and Suzy for a
little bit just to get that off the
ground. And he's got, you know, in his
book Radical Candor, right? And I And
that's actually what drew me to Jack.
Now, we can all talk about Jack and
capitalism and, you know, people hate
him these days, but I, you know, back
then his whole mentality of radical
candor was was music to my ears because
me, as somebody who just couldn't fit in
corporate because I I have zero filter,
right? And And And I And I don't want a
filter. And I actually don't want people
around me to have a filter because I
don't think with a filter I don't think
you can get anything done. But
everybody's got, you know, sensitivity
and all that other stuff. So,
how did you shift specifically the IT
culture that was scared to talk, limited
ownership, in a organization that was
running like, "Hey, we can do anything
and sorry, IT, you're going to have to
figure this out." Was it Was it
deliberate on your end to to to address
that issue coming in?
And how did you make that shift of
I'm scared to own anything to radical
honesty, let's go.
>> Mhm. Trust.
>> Trust, okay.
>> Right. Yeah.
>> I love that.
>> So, the most important thing I did was
hire leaders who
who believed the same thing, right? And
wanted to build teams that could trust
each other because I've seen it.
Innovation happens when you get teams of
people that trust each other enough to
take risks, to admit when they fail, to
try again, to speak up,
right? So, that every person can speak
up and say their ideas, what's on their
mind, what's not working. Um that's
where I believe innovation really
happens is when you can build teams that
are so highly performant that the you
know, that there's no politics, right?
We're just here to climb that mountain
in front of us together.
>> Was there a moment or an example where
you saw that shift of and so there's the
hiring of new people that fit the
profile. So great, right? So this is new
people that you're hiring builds the
trust up front. But then there's the

here that is still probably

before and and how it was received or

it was was approached?
>> Yeah, very I'll give you a very very

>> Mhm.
>> [clears throat]

it?
>> [snorts]
>> And
whenever I would ask that the room would
just go quiet.
And you know, and I'm new and I've just

okay, let's get going, right? All the
reasons why.
>> [snorts]
>> And he finally said to me, Julie,
your predecessor was a bare metals
person.

it's everything just made sense to me.
I'm like, okay, now I understand the
starting point.
>> Okay.
>> Right?
>> And explain for those people who are

you clarify

legacy of SaaS, which is a weird thing
to say.
>> Three years old, yeah.
>> Right, exactly. These legacy SaaS
companies. And and quite frankly who are

for this? Why can't I just do it myself?
And obviously there's sales skills
there. But what are the successful
companies going to do coming out of this
right now as opposed to just get run

companies coming up.
>> Yeah.
I think it's a great question. And I
know that that's what

right now. If I was advising, I would
say be real.
>> Right.
>> Right. Understand, do you offer
something that is competitive advantage?

understand why your customers need it.
And focus on that, but also understand
what your competition is, which is, you
know, uh
an AI engineer
with a bunch of tokens.
>> Mhm.
>> So, [clears throat]
um
you know, you have to understand that
and then price accordingly.
>> Mhm.
>> Because if you have a pricing model that
you know, that gets you a piece of the

>> Yeah.
>> So, I really I think that the whole

>> Mhm.
>> Um and do you understand that the world
has changed? So, you can't price price

>> So, how do you make decisions with with
things that are moving so
so rapidly right now.
>> Yeah.
>> How do you There's There's I talk a lot
about this book called JOLT Effect,
right? Which is the Challenger Sale,
Challenger Customer, and and then the
third one is JOLT Effect, and it talks
about the the problem that we're all
faced with right now, which is you know,
every sales rep says, "Oh, you know, no
decision is our biggest, you know,
competitor, right?" People not making a
decision. But what JOLT Effect
highlights is it's not no decision, it's
indecision. And the reason is is that
things are moving so fast right now, and
there there's just so many other options
that people are scared to make
decisions. They're They're Very few
people get fired for making bad
decisions. Oh, I'm sorry, very few few
people get fired for not making
decisions. They get fired for making bad
decisions. So people are just kind of in
a like, "Ah." That's why sales cycles
are taking too long. And And so how do
you
like
from an innovation still steering you
back to innovation,
>> [sighs]
>> how do you know what to invest in? If
you're a CEO right now, if you're a CIO
right now, knowing that I mean, just in
my little world, I'm like, "Oh, cool,
I'm going to do this." And then
literally
3 weeks later Anthropic releases some
new thing, ChatGPT creates whatever it
is, and whatever I was just about to
create or do is now irrelevant because I
can do it in GPT or in in AI. So how are
How is decision-making
changing right now? And what is the lens
you have to look at right now to
green-light anything at this point?
>> Yeah. I'll give an example I have a
real-life example of a company that I'm
advising right now, right? So they have
a really old legacy stack.
>> Mhm.
>> And uh their choice is

>> Mhm.
>> Um
the the things that you have to weigh in
that is like
can you really
like is it as easy as you think it is?
>> Right.
>> Cuz there is advantage to going to a

company that is, you know, let's say
under a billion dollars,
you can't get the talent
>> Right.
>> that you need to be able to create your

need to as a business grows. So, there
is still a need for these
really flexible um SaaS solutions that

their team focus on, you know, instead

commerce, take payments, etc. Like,
that's all figured out. And now all they

do those really, really well.
>> Yeah.
>> So, I think, you know, the the the
decisions are still the same. It's just
that there's another option and this
option of
doing it yourself.
>> Mhm.
>> Um
But but but but it's not
>> Right.
>> Yeah, it's not it's not a magic pill.
>> No. I mean, that's it's funny. When I
was again, going back to Thrive, in my
these IT services, we did infrastructure
and we would always bump into engineers,
you know, software engineers, who were
really smart. And they could absolutely
manage the infrastructure. But that's
not what they got paid for. That's not
what they what their specialty was.
Their specialty was creating the
software that the company could make
money on, right? And the infrastructure
got in their way. So, it was always an
interesting dynamic of talk of having
that conversation to be like, yeah, I
know you could, but is this the best use
of your time, right? The build versus
buy has always been around. And I think
now it's just more challenging because
the perception is it's so much easier to
build. So, it's not just the the IT
nerds, if you will, that are like, oh, I
could do that. And, you know, as a sales
guy here, I'm like, yeah, you You do
that. Do don't know. I don't know if you
can or can't. Like, cuz I don't know
what the skills are to But now some
idiot like me can be like, "Wait a
minute. I just built a crazy app that
does this, and it took me 2 minutes of,
you know, half an hour of AI coding
here, and it came out pretty damn good.
It does everything that I wanted to. So,
can I just do this?" Obviously not
knowing the details of the integrations
and all the stuff that it should do in
the security and all that fun stuff. But
I think right now it's it's it's the
same question. Build versus buy is just
now it's a little bit more clouded
because you have non-technical people
seeing what the possibilities are and
and playing with it at home in a lot of
ways, like me.
>> Yeah.
>> So.
>> It got
>> [clears throat]
>> It got more tangible.
>> Yeah.
>> Right. Whereas engineering has been a
little bit of a mystery.
>> Mhm.
>> I remember when I when I first told um
the someone that I wanted to be a CIO,
uh this was the former CIO of Nordstrom.
He He took me out to lunch, and he said,
"I want you to really understand I want
to understand why you want to do this,
and I want you to understand some
things." And one of the things he said
is, "This is a lonely job. Nobody
understands what you do,
and everybody needs you.
And if anything breaks, they're mad at
you."
>> Yeah.
>> So,
you know, I think with AI, people like
they they get to see into that box a
little bit. Like, "Oh, I can see. Look,
I just, you know, look what I just made,
and I made my own website in, you know,
in very little time."
But it doesn't take away the complexity
under the under the hood, if you will,
right? You still need senior engineers
who know what they're doing and are
building for the future, not just, you
know, built putting out some code,
building on top of it, building on top
of it, and then you have something
that's unsustainable.
>> Yeah, I think you had said something
that AI is a magnifying glass, not a
solve-all. And I And that's true. Like,
I I thought AI was going to cure the
garbage in, garbage out problem. Like,
ah, whatever. Just throw it in there.
It'll make it a clean it all up, right?"
And I couldn't have been s- Further That
couldn't be further from the truth. If
you have garbage in,
it's going to build on garbage, and it's
just going to make it exponentially
worse, as a matter of fact. So, it's I
see that with messaging and I and
everything else, and I can't imagine
what it looks like from an actual
infrastructure and development
standpoint.
>> Well, the other part of that is that
what AI reveals
is the dysfunction in the company.
>> Mhm.
>> So, if you're you know, you're trying to
automate a process, and yet it takes 30
people in a room to make a decision,
those is it you can't automate that.
>> Yeah.
>> Uh um you know, the hoarding of data,
right? Where data is power, I'm going to
hold on to it. You lose that power as
soon as you know, you make that avail-
data available to everyone else. So,
that That's part of the reason why I say
AI reveals your company.
Because it doesn't it doesn't just come
in and
spread some magic pixel dust around it,
and everything's all of a sudden
perfect.
>> Yeah.
>> As a matter of fact, it it exposes the
the things about your company faster.
>> Yeah.
And I It's the same parallel in a sales
process. It do It just exposes the the
process, the bad parts of it. It also
exposes the bad parts of sales reps,
because it it it gets it all, you know,
so many reps are are jumping towards the
answer, and not like, you know, they're
putting I say all the time, we've gone
from a search engine to an answer
engine, and that's a dangerous thing.
Because the search engine at least gave
us a perception of choice, and we had to
have some critical thinking to read
through a few articles, and maybe pick
something that was our source of truth.
Now, it just gives us the answer.
And if you're curious, which you
mentioned a couple of times, which I
think is the superpower of the future
here, it is a superpower. Hey, tell me
more about that. Where'd you learn that?
I don't know. Give me a different
perspective here. Source check that,
please, whatever. But, unfortunately,
the vast majority of people are just
lazy, and they're going to take that
answer, copy, paste it, and go. And I I
think like all that grind that I did
over the years and everything else,
that perspective, that context is so
critical cuz now that I'm playing with
it, I call BS on it all the time. I'm
like,
"Uh
I don't think that's right." Oh, oh,
sorry, John, you're right. And it's
like, "Wait a minute." Like, you know,
somebody without the experience would
just accept that. Sounds good.
>> Yeah.
>> And that's the danger I think we're in
here with with this. Wow, it made it It
sounds really smart. It looks like it
did the research, but if I don't have
the critical thinking, if I don't have
the curiosity, if I don't have the
context,
>> it gets off all the time. I mean, I love
to say um cite your sources.
>> Yes.
>> Then I go, "Oh,
uh hold on a second."
>> ChatGPT
is my source.
>> Yeah.
>> [laughter]
>> But no, even telling even telling um the

>> So, let's let's finish up on the book

>> A,

>> Yeah, I

um

needed to write a book.
>> Mhm.
>> And it planted the seed.
>> Mhm.
>> And I first I thought, well, you know,

do. I don't have time to write a book.

stories. And then, I um you know, I
stood back and I like, what do these
say?

uh interesting process to go through and
like, what do all my stories say? What
is that through line
>> Mhm.
>> that um you know, that communicates the
things that I believe.
So, the the byline of the book is real
transformation requires human, not just
artificial intelligence.
>> Mhm.
>> [clears throat]
>> And so, it's it's my view on
transformation
on a on a human level, on a global
level, on a company level. And really
what it takes to influence and to lead.
So, um if I were something I would say
for the one-liner that I would say to
CIOs right now is that
transformations
require like, technology is the easy
part. It's the humans
uh that you need to lead through it
and help in that change. So,
it's um I'm really excited for it to
come out.
>> Yeah.
>> Uh,
and I called it Chief Impact Officer
because that's what I desire in the
world, right? I I want to have impact.
>> Mhm.
>> That's what and that's what the book is
about.
>> Love it.
Uh, what's your opti- last question for
you? I always try to end on an
optimistic note, especially in this
crazy world of AI. What is kind of what
are you most optimistic about,
specifically related to AI? Because
there's a lot of doomsday Terminator
conversations about this thing's going
to wake up and take over everything.
Jobs are going to be gone and we can
leave that to other other conversations.
But, what what are you most optimistic
about um, with AI and how it's impacting
us as a society, as business, and
everything else?
>> Yeah.
I'm teaching a class right now at the
University of Washington, a master's
seminar, and it's just so much fun
>> Yeah.
>> to be in with the students and to have
dialogue and conversation. And it's a
scary time for them, for sure.
>> Yep.
That's what it is.
>> But,
the human skills,
like we are still humans
>> Yep.
>> that are still controlling the planet.
And the skills that make us unique,
right? The
um,

really from AI or are they pressures
from these company to

really is gained by AI right now, it's

relevant? I think it is the human
things. It's leadership and you know,

>> Yeah.
Yeah, I think it's

just want to have do their job. And and

keep that in mind that we're all not
creators and we have to create for the
rest of people so that everybody can
have purpose. Everybody can make a
difference. Everybody can have that

>> Yeah.
